title: The Toledo Torso Murders: A Hidden Series 🕯

segments.voice: Between 1922 and 1935, a series of dismembered bodies appeared in and around Toledo, Ohio. Their torsos were left in marshes, riverbanks, and railway corridors. The killings have been overshadowed by the more famous Cleveland Torso Killer cases — but Toledo investigators believe the same hand may have been at work.

The earliest known Toledo torso case was discovered in 1922. The remains of an unidentified woman were found in a marshy area near Maumee Bay. The body had been dismembered with surgical precision. The head and hands were never recovered.
Toledo police at the time had limited resources for forensic investigation. The case was filed and largely forgotten until similar bodies appeared in subsequent years.
Three more torso cases were recorded in the Toledo area between 1925 and 1932. Each victim was unidentified. Each had been dismembered. Each was found in or near water.

By 1935, when the Cleveland killings began drawing national attention, Toledo authorities reviewed their unsolved torso cases. Detective Charles Riddle of the Toledo Police Department began the comparison work.
Riddle traveled to Cleveland to consult with Detective Peter Merylo. The two compared notes for two days. They identified at least four similarities between the Toledo and Cleveland series.
The shared traits included precision in dismemberment, draining of blood before disposal, removal of identifying features such as hands and heads, and use of waterways or marshes for body disposal.

Here's where the Toledo cases gained renewed importance. In 1936, an unidentified male torso was found in the marshes near the Toledo riverfront. The cuts matched the technique used in two known Cleveland victims.

Merylo formally requested that Toledo investigators consider whether the Cleveland Torso Killer was traveling between cities. He hypothesized the killer used the rail lines connecting Cleveland and Toledo to move regularly.

Toledo's connection to the rail network was central to Merylo's theory. The same routes used by Depression-era rail-riders could carry a man with a vehicle and a private space for dismemberment. The body-disposal sites in both cities were near rail corridors.

Some researchers have expanded the geographic theory to include New Castle, Pennsylvania, where torso bodies were found between 1923 and 1939, and Pittsburgh, where similar cases were recorded in the late 1930s.
If a single killer operated across this corridor, the victim count could approach 20 or more across Ohio and Pennsylvania. No single investigator was ever assigned the cross-jurisdictional case.

Toledo identified one possible victim by name in 1936 — Robert Smith, a railroad worker whose torso was found in a culvert near Toledo's industrial east side. His head and hands were not recovered. His identification was based on a tattoo and witness statements.

Smith had been seen in Cleveland in early 1936 before his disappearance. The geographic and timeline overlap with the active Cleveland killings is documented in the Cuyahoga County and Lucas County files.

After 1938, the Toledo torso cases stopped. The Cleveland killings stopped at almost the same time. The simultaneous cessation reinforced the cross-city theory among the original investigators, even if it could not be proved.
Detective Riddle wrote in 1939 that he believed the Toledo and Cleveland cases were linked. His written assessment is held at the Toledo-Lucas County Public Library in the Local History collection.

Riddle did not name a specific suspect. The Cleveland investigators had focused on Francis Sweeney, the surgeon Eliot Ness had interrogated. Sweeney's voluntary commitment to a veterans' hospital in 1938 coincided with the end of both Cleveland and Toledo killings.

Some Toledo researchers have argued that an additional suspect — a slaughterhouse worker named Frank Polillo, no relation to the Cleveland victim Florence Polillo — should be considered. Frank Polillo lived in Toledo and worked at a packing plant. He had medical knowledge from a brief stint as an army medic. He was investigated and cleared by Toledo police.

DNA evidence does not exist for the Toledo cases. Bodies were buried in unmarked plots in Lucas County cemeteries. Records from the original autopsies are partial. Most of what remains is documentary.

Local historians have traced the burial sites of two of the unidentified Toledo victims. Volunteers have placed simple markers at their graves. The graves are in Forest Cemetery and at Maumee Cemetery.

The Toledo Torso Murders are studied as part of regional Ohio crime history. Researchers at Bowling Green State University and the University of Toledo have produced theses and papers analyzing the case files.

Forensic archaeologists have visited some of the original dump sites. The marshlands where bodies were found are largely undeveloped. They remain quiet — one of the strange aspects of the case is how unchanged the geography has remained.
The Lucas County Sheriff's Department maintains the historical Toledo Torso Murders files. They are accessible to credentialed researchers and to next-of-kin.

If you are descended from a Toledo or Cleveland resident who disappeared between 1922 and 1938, you can request a comparison check against the unidentified-remains files at both county sheriff offices. DNA from descendants can sometimes match historic case markers.
